name: WebSocket server reflects an arbitrary requested subprotocol
description: Performs the WebSocket upgrade requesting a subprotocol the server has no reason to support (Sec-WebSocket-Protocol: chat.attacker). When the server echoes that exact value back in the 101 response instead of selecting from its own supported set (or omitting the header), it blindly accepts client-dictated subprotocols. Per RFC 6455 the server must choose one of the client-offered subprotocols that it actually supports, or none; reflecting an arbitrary one indicates the negotiation isn't validated, which can steer the connection into an unintended message framing / handler and is a building block for subprotocol-confusion attacks. The predicate matches the 101 plus the reflected attacker subprotocol.

vulnerability.remediation: Only ever select a Sec-WebSocket-Protocol the server actually implements. Never echo the client's requested subprotocol unconditionally — negotiate against a fixed server-side supported set and, if none match, omit the header (and optionally reject the connection). ASP.NET Core: pass an explicit supported value to `AcceptWebSocketAsync("your.known.subprotocol")` only when the client offered it, rather than forwarding `WebSocketRequestedProtocols[0]` verbatim. Verify with a handshake offering a bogus subprotocol — the 101 response must not echo it back.
